agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A feature request proposes adding a 'confirmed' status to the Quotes bounded context. However, the term 'confirmed' is already established in the Orders bounded context with a precise, different meaning. This creates a domain language collision where the same term would carry contradictory semantics across two closely related contexts in the same business process pipeline (quotes flow into orders).","bounded_context":"Quotes / Orders boundary — the collision sits exactly at the seam where quotes convert to orders.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Confirmed Order","definition":"ESTABLISHED: An order that has been paid and is queued for fulfillment. A confirmed order is immutable — no further changes are permitted."},{"term":"Confirmed Quote (PROPOSED)","definition":"PROPOSED: A quote where the customer has verbally agreed to terms but payment has NOT yet occurred. Mutable status — still subject to change before payment."},{"term":"Quote","definition":"A priced proposal presented to a customer, subject to acceptance and conversion to an order."},{"term":"Immutable","definition":"Once a domain object reaches this state, no field may be modified. This is an integrity guarantee, not a preference."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"'Confirmed' in the Orders context means paid and immutable. 'Confirmed' in the proposed Quotes context means verbally agreed but unpaid and still mutable. These are contradictory definitions of the same term within a single business domain pipeline.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Domain language integrity is a hard invariant. If the same term means 'paid + immutable' in one context and 'unpaid + mutable' in an adjacent context on the same pipeline, the ubiquitous language is corrupted. Developers, support agents, and business stakeholders WILL confuse these, leading to data integrity violations (e.g., treating a 'confirmed' quote as if payment occurred).","testable_assertion":"The term 'confirmed' must have exactly one unambiguous meaning when used without a qualifying context prefix across the quote-to-order lifecycle."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"A confirmed order is immutable — no modifications after confirmation.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is established domain truth encoding a payment-finality guarantee. If violated, the system permits changes to paid, queued-for-fulfillment orders, which breaks financial integrity.","testable_assertion":"Given an order in 'confirmed' status, when any field modification is attempted, then the system must reject the modification."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"A verbally agreed quote has not yet resulted in payment and remains mutable until converted to a paid order.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a business process rule about quote lifecycle. The mutability window is a policy decision — a VP could decide verbal agreement locks certain fields while leaving others editable.","testable_assertion":"Given a quote with verbal customer agreement, when a field is modified before payment, then the system permits the modification."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A developer writes code checking `status == 'confirmed'` without specifying whether it's a quote or order, and the query spans both tables or a shared status enum.","expected_behavior":"This must not be possible. If the same term is used, shared queries, reporting dashboards, and status filters will silently conflate paid-immutable orders with unpaid-mutable quotes."},{"scenario":"A support agent sees 'Confirmed' in the UI and tells a customer their order is being fulfilled, when in fact it's only a verbally agreed quote with no payment.","expected_behavior":"The UI must make the distinction unambiguous. With the same term, this error is inevitable."},{"scenario":"A quote is 'confirmed' (verbal agreement) and the customer disputes they ever agreed. There is no payment as evidence.","expected_behavior":"Unlike order confirmation (backed by payment), quote 'confirmation' has no verifiable artifact, making the status legally and operationally weaker — yet it carries the same authoritative name."},{"scenario":"Analytics or reporting queries count all 'confirmed' records as revenue-committed.","expected_behavior":"Confirmed orders represent committed revenue; confirmed quotes do not. Same-term reporting will overstate the pipeline or misclassify revenue stage."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"HIGH RISK — Conflating 'confirmed' (paid) with 'confirmed' (verbal agreement) in reporting, dashboards, or downstream systems could cause revenue misstatement, premature fulfillment of unpaid quotes, or audit findings.","operational":"HIGH RISK — Support, fulfillment, and sales teams will encounter persistent confusion. Training materials, runbooks, and internal communication all break when a core term is overloaded.","user":"MEDIUM RISK — Customers shown a 'confirmed' status may reasonably believe their order is paid and in progress, when it's actually a quote awaiting payment. This erodes trust.","risk":"CRITICAL — Domain language corruption is one of the highest-leverage failure modes in software. It doesn't cause one bug; it causes a class of bugs that recur indefinitely and are hard to diagnose because everyone 'knows' what confirmed means — they just each know something different.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Sales Agents","impact":"Will use 'confirmed' to mean verbal agreement. Will inevitably miscommunicate with fulfillment team who interprets it as paid."},{"group":"Fulfillment Team","impact":"May begin processing 'confirmed' quotes as if payment has been received, leading to shipped-but-unpaid orders."},{"group":"Finance / Accounting","impact":"Revenue recognition and pipeline reporting corrupted if 'confirmed' conflates paid orders with verbal agreements."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"Shared enums, status checks, and queries become semantic landmines. Every `if status == confirmed` is now ambiguous."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"May receive mixed signals about whether their purchase is finalized or still pending payment."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"The spec contains a direct contradiction: 'confirmed' is defined as paid+immutable in the established domain, but the feature request redefines it as unpaid+mutable in an adjacent context on the same pipeline. This is not an ambiguity — it is a semantic collision. Confidence is low because acceptance criteria cannot be written until the naming conflict is resolved.","high_confidence_areas":["The existing definition of 'confirmed order' is clear and established","The proposed 'confirmed quote' directly contradicts the established semantics","This will cause real operational confusion if shipped as-is"],"low_confidence_areas":["What the business actually wants to call the verbal-agreement status","Whether the quote lifecycle has other intermediate states that should be considered","Whether there are existing integrations or reports that key on the term 'confirmed'"],"assumptions":["Quotes and orders exist in the same system or tightly coupled systems","The term 'confirmed' is visible in UIs, APIs, and reporting — not just internal code"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The term 'confirmed' is already established to mean 'paid and immutable' for orders. The feature request redefines 'confirmed' for quotes to mean 'verbally agreed, unpaid, and mutable.' These are mutually exclusive definitions of the same domain term on the same business pipeline (quote → order). This is a ubiquitous language violation that will cause systemic confusion across engineering, operations, sales, and finance.","affected_stakeholders":["Sales Agents","Fulfillment Team","Finance","Engineering","Customers"],"options":["OPTION A: Rename the quote status to a distinct term that does not collide. Candidates: 'Accepted' (customer accepted terms), 'Agreed' (verbal agreement reached), 'Committed' (customer committed but not yet paid). Recommended: 'Accepted' — it clearly conveys customer agreement without implying payment or immutability.","OPTION B: Rename the existing order status away from 'confirmed' to resolve the collision from the other direction. NOT RECOMMENDED — this is established, in-production terminology with existing integrations.","OPTION C: Keep both as 'confirmed' but qualify everywhere (e.g., 'Quote Confirmed' vs 'Order Confirmed'). NOT RECOMMENDED — qualifiers are forgotten, especially in code, queries, and verbal communication."],"recommendation":"OPTION A — Rename the new quote status to 'Accepted.' It accurately describes the business event (customer accepted the quote terms), clearly distinguishes from order confirmation (which implies payment), and preserves the established ubiquitous language. I will not write acceptance criteria until the naming contradiction is resolved."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Ubiquitous language collision: 'confirmed' cannot mean both 'paid + immutable' (orders) and 'verbally agreed + unpaid + mutable' (quotes) in the same domain.","business_rule_broken":"BR-1: The term 'confirmed' must have exactly one unambiguous meaning across the quote-to-order lifecycle."}]}
